title: Money Signals
summary: Money signals track how households, banks, apps, and regulators shape everyday financial life.

items.name: Household budgets remain under pressure
items.summary: Living costs and rent keep shaping financial behaviour.
items.whyItMatters: Households are making money decisions under tighter margins.

items.name: Digital payments keep displacing cash
items.summary: Wallets and instant transfers are becoming the default.
items.whyItMatters: Payment systems are changing how people think about spending.

items.name: Banks
items.summary: Banks shape lending, deposits, and the cost of money.
items.whyItMatters: Bank behaviour affects household choices and business investment.

items.name: Fintech apps
items.summary: New tools are making budgeting and payments more visible.
items.whyItMatters: Interface design now affects money behaviour directly.

items.name: RBA
items.summary: Interest rates and inflation data change the money environment.
items.whyItMatters: Central bank settings shape borrowing and saving decisions.

items.name: ASIC
items.summary: Consumer money rules and disclosure remain important.
items.whyItMatters: Clear rules protect people from hidden costs and scams.

items.name: Morgan Housel
items.summary: Influential writer on behaviour and wealth.
items.whyItMatters: He helps people think about money less romantically and more realistically.

items.name: Ramit Sethi
items.whyItMatters: His work keeps focusing attention on system design and habits.

items.name: Is financial literacy enough?
items.summary: Some argue system design matters more than individual discipline.
items.whyItMatters: This debate changes how people think about policy and personal responsibility.

items.name: Can money apps improve behavior?
items.summary: Products can nudge users toward saving or toward spending.
items.whyItMatters: Interface design is now part of financial life.
